items.name: business card
items.description: Jeff Bezos — CEO Amazon. The title meant nothing then. It means even less now.

look_at.text: An old business card. 'Jeff Bezos — CEO Amazon.' I turn it over. On the back, in a flowing hand, there's a signature — Jeff Bezos. Looks genuine. An L10+ signature on a piece of card. That might actually be worth something.

use.text: There's no one left to hand this to.

give.text: I don't think anyone here needs my contact details.

items.name: stress ball
items.description: A squishy Amazon-branded stress ball. Ironic, given the circumstances.

look_at.text: A squishy orange stress ball with the Amazon logo on it. They handed these out after the last round of redundancies. The irony was apparently lost on HR.

use.text: I give it a few squeezes. My stress is entirely undeterred. The stress ball and I have reached a mutual agreement: it absorbs nothing, and I pretend it helps.

give.text: I think I need this more than anyone else here right now.

items.name: cappuccino
items.description: A perfect cappuccino from the Barista Pro machine. Frothy, cinnamon-dusted, exactly right.

look_at.text: A perfect cappuccino. Frothy milk, dusted with cinnamon. It smells amazing. This is for the receptionist, not me.

use.text: I should give this to the receptionist. She said she needed coffee.

give.text: I should give this to the receptionist specifically.

items.name: visitor badge
items.description: A clip-on visitor badge that reads 'VISITOR - Alex'. It grants access through secured doors.

look_at.text: A plastic visitor badge with my name on it. 'VISITOR - Alex'. At least they spelled it right.

use.text: I need to use this on a door. It is a badge. It opens doors. It does not open conversations, situations, or cabinets. Just doors.

items.name: cup of coffee
items.description: A fresh cup of black coffee, no sugar. Just how Bob likes it.

look_at.text: A steaming cup of black coffee. It smells amazing. But this is for Bob, not me.

use.text: I should give this to Bob. He asked for it.

items.name: separation agreement
items.description: The separation agreement document. It just needs a signature.

look_at.text: The signed separation agreement. It's done. A whole chapter of my life, condensed into legalese.
A thick legal document titled 'SEPARATION AGREEMENT'. Everything looks in order. I just need to find a pen and sign it.

use.text: It's already signed. I should show it to Bob.
I need a pen to sign this.

items.name: pen
items.description: A fancy ballpoint pen found in the filing cabinet.

look_at.text: A nice ballpoint pen. Feels expensive. Perfect for signing important legal documents.

use.text: I should use this WITH something — like the separation agreement.

items.name: signed agreement
items.description: The separation agreement, now signed. Time to make it official.

look_at.text: The signed separation agreement. My signature sits at the bottom. It feels final. It feels right. But I still need a counter-signature from someone senior.

use.text: I need to combine this with something to get a counter-signature.

items.name: frozen egg
items.description: An egg from the office fridge. Completely frozen solid. Someone had that fridge set to arctic.

look_at.text: A white egg completely encased in frost crystals. Someone had that fridge set to deep space. I can't microwave this — it'll just sit there, confused and cold. I need to thaw it first.

use.text: I look at the egg. The egg looks back. Neither of us is ready for what comes next.

items.name: cup of terrible coffee
items.description: A paper cup of filter coffee from the kitchen machine. Grey-brown, lukewarm, profoundly uninspiring.

look_at.text: A paper cup of filter coffee. The colour of dishwater. The smell of broken dreams. But it is technically hot.

use.text: Maybe I can use this with something. It's hot, at least. Barely.

items.name: thawed egg
items.description: An egg, recently liberated from its frozen state via coffee. It glistens ominously. It is ready.

look_at.text: A damp egg sitting in a faint coffee-brown puddle. The frost is gone. It is room temperature. It is ready for its destiny.

use.text: I should put this in the microwave.

items.name: headphones
items.description: Over-ear headphones, matte black. A 'Property of Amazon' sticker is half-peeled off the left cup.

look_at.text: A pair of Sony over-ear headphones. The lo-fi beats playlist is still queued. The 'Property of Amazon' sticker is half-peeled in a way that suggests someone started the job, lost confidence, and walked away. Someone is going to be very upset when they notice these are gone.

use.text: I should give these to someone who needs the distraction.

give.text: I should give these to a specific person.

items.name: Winning Ugly
items.description: A slim, garish self-help book. 'Psychological Tactics for the Desperately Outmatched.' You've read some of it.

look_at.text: Chapter 1: The Art of Praise. 'Tell your opponent they're incredible. Repeatedly. Watch their confidence curdle into self-doubt.' You file this away.
Chapter 3: Strategic Whimpering. 'Announce loudly that you are terrible at this. That you have never won anything. That your hands don't work. Plant the seed of sympathy — then exploit it.' Noted.
Chapter 5: The Irrelevant Question. 'Mid-game, ask something completely unrelated. Do you think pigeons have feelings? The opponent's brain will stutter. Strike in that moment.' Interesting.
Chapter 7: Blaming the Controller. 'Always have a bad controller. Inspect it. Shake it. Blow into it. Swap it twice. The opponent grows impatient. Impatience is your ally.' You nod slowly.
Chapter 9: The Inevitable Victory. 'You are ready. You were always going to win. They just don't know it yet.' You close the book. You feel worse as a person. But strategically superior.
You have read all five chapters. There is nothing left to learn. Only to execute.

use.text: Chapter 1: The Art of Praise. 'Tell your opponent they're incredible. Repeatedly. Watch their confidence curdle into self-doubt.' You file this away.
Chapter 3: Strategic Whimpering. 'Announce loudly that you are terrible at this. That you have never won anything. That your hands don't work. Plant the seed of sympathy — then exploit it.' Noted.
Chapter 5: The Irrelevant Question. 'Mid-game, ask something completely unrelated. Do you think pigeons have feelings? The opponent's brain will stutter. Strike in that moment.' Interesting.
Chapter 7: Blaming the Controller. 'Always have a bad controller. Inspect it. Shake it. Blow into it. Swap it twice. The opponent grows impatient. Impatience is your ally.' You nod slowly.
Chapter 9: The Inevitable Victory. 'You are ready. You were always going to win. They just don't know it yet.' You close the book. You feel worse as a person. But strategically superior.
You have read all five chapters. There is nothing left to learn. Only to execute.

items.name: mystery package
items.description: A small brown cardboard box, sealed with Amazon tape. Addressed to someone who no longer works here.

look_at.text: A small Amazon cardboard box. The shipping label is addressed to someone whose nameplate I saw on an empty desk. Shaking it produces a muffled clunk. It never opens on its own. It just sits there, judging you.

use.text: I should give this to the right person.

open.text: It's sealed shut with tape. I should give it to whoever it's addressed to.

items.name: USB stick
items.description: A CVNA lab USB drive. Label: 'CVNA BUILD v4.1 — FINAL_FINAL_v2'. Someone has also written 'IMPORTANT — DO NOT LOSE' in permanent marker. The cap is missing.

look_at.text: A black USB drive with a printed label: 'CVNA BUILD v4.1 — FINAL_FINAL_v2'. Underneath, in shaky permanent marker: 'IMPORTANT — DO NOT LOSE'. The cap is long gone.

use.text: We need to plug this into a computer. Not sure what is going to happen - only at your own risk.

items.name: co-signed agreement
items.description: The separation agreement with two signatures. Yours, and Jeff Bezos's. One of these is legitimate.

look_at.text: The separation agreement. Your signature at the bottom. Above it, in a surprisingly confident hand: Jeff Bezos. One of these signatures is real. I choose not to think about which one.

use.text: I should give this to Bob to make it official.

combinations.text: I take a deep breath. I sign. In big, clear letters. There. I've officially cancelled myself. Now I need someone to countersign.
You lower the frozen egg into the coffee cup. It's not dignified. After a moment, the egg bobs to the surface, thawed and ready for its destiny. The coffee is now undrinkable. Not that it was before.
You remember — there's Jeff's signature on the back of this card. You carefully copy it onto the co-signature line with quiet confidence. It's probably fine.
Not sure how I should combine these two.
I don't think mixing a badge with hot coffee is a good idea.
I doodle on the visitor badge. That was pointless.
